title: Renowned winemaker reveals new focus on namesake brand following departure from Endeavour
summary:
Endeavour Group has confirmed that David Bicknell has departed from his position as chief winemaker at Oakridge Wines in the Yarra Valley. Daily Wine News reached out to Bicknell to discuss his future plans. “I have a small family winemaking business, Bicknell FC, which we started in 2011. So far, it’s been a bit of a busman’s holiday as I was completely consumed at Oakridge. The only current plan is to concentrate on developing that business, although I will be happy to talk anyone interested in my services,” he shared. Bicknell’s wines can be purchased directly via his website or through its distributor Real Wines. David Bicknell led winemaking at Oakridge Wines for nearly 25 years, beginning his time there in 2002. Under his leadership, Oakridge was awarded the number one winery in Australia by both the Halliday Wine Companion and The Real Review in 2023. He announced his departure via a post to Instagram on Monday.

The post Renowned winemaker reveals new focus on namesake brand following departure from Endeavour appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
David Bicknell is willing to speak with anyone interested in his services and will focus on his own wine brand. Image supplied

By Kyra Trussell

Endeavour Group has confirmed that David Bicknell has departed from his position as chief winemaker at Oakridge Wines in the Yarra Valley.

Daily Wine News reached out to Bicknell to discuss his future plans.

“I have a small family winemaking business, Bicknell FC, which we started in 2011. So far, it’s been a bit of a busman’s holiday as I was completely consumed at Oakridge. The only current plan is to concentrate on developing that business, although I will be happy to talk anyone interested in my services,” he shared.

Bicknell’s wines can be purchased directly via his website or through its distributor Real Wines.

David Bicknell led winemaking at Oakridge Wines for nearly 25 years, beginning his time there in 2002. Under his leadership, Oakridge was awarded the number one winery in Australia by both the Halliday Wine Companion and The Real Review in 2023.

He announced his departure via a post to Instagram on Monday.

“It’s been an amazing ride, taking what was broken winery to the top of the industry with an unwavering commitment to quality and plenty of hard graft. Some really good times with some really good people.

“They say you never know you’re going to get boned until you do. And so, it is. Thankfully, I leave a highly trained, capable and talented team for the next piece of history. Your support for them would mean a lot to me. Love my team,” he wrote.

As reported by The Australian, Bicknell does not wish to comment further on the departure and told the outlet simply, “I did not choose to leave Oakridge”.

Speaking to Daily Wine News, a spokesperson for Endeavour Group commented on Bicknell’s departure and the future of leadership at Oakridge.

“David Bicknell has departed Oakridge following more than 20 years of service at the winery. He will be succeeded by senior winemaker, Tim Dexter.

“Tim has worked with David since 2023 and will continue the close partnership with award-winning viticulturist Steve Faulkner.

“David helped build Oakridge into the brand it is today while mentoring the next generation of winemakers who will carry the winery forward.

“The Oakridge team and operations are continuing business as usual,” they said.

Michael Fragos, chief winemaker at Chapel Hill in McLaren Vale, has also departed from his position.

The spokesperson for Endeavour thanked Fragos for his time at Chapel Hill.

“Michael Fragos has made a valuable contribution to Chapel Hill. Endeavour Group thanks Michael for his service and dedication.”

Michael Fragos began working at Chapel Hill as senior winemaker in 2004 and was eventually appointed to the position of chief winemaker in 2013. Notably, Fragos received the title of ‘Winemaker of the Year’ at the London International Wine & Spirit Competition (IWSC) in 2007.

Fragos has not commented publicly on his departure as of the publication of this article.

Endeavour recently appointed Colliers for the sale of Oakridge Wines and Elders for the sale of Chapel Hill.

title: 2026 makes for Australia’s smallest vintage in more than 25 years
summary:
According to Wine Australia’s National Vintage Report released today, Australia’s 2026 winegrape crush fell to 1.27 million tonnes – its smallest since 2000 – but the reduced national crop “did not result in stronger grape prices”. The national estimated crush is down 300,000 tonnes (19 per cent) from the 2025 crush and 25 per cent below its 10-year average of 1.69 million tonnes. This equates to around 33 million fewer 9-litre cases of wine. Peter Bailey, Wine Australia manager for market insights, said that this year’s crush reflected both seasonal pressures and a broader response to changed consumer demand. “A number of significant seasonal challenges, including flooding in the inland regions, had an impact on production, but the main driver of this lower crush is a deliberate adjustment in response to changing market conditions. “There have now been four vintages in a row below the long-term average, suggesting an underlying reset in the tonnage of grapes required by winemakers to meet changing global demand,” he said.

The post 2026 makes for Australia’s smallest vintage in more than 25 years appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The annual winegrape crush for Australia is the smallest its been since 2000. Images supplied

According to Wine Australia’s National Vintage Report released today, Australia’s 2026 winegrape crush fell to 1.27 million tonnes – its smallest since 2000 – but the reduced national crop “did not result in stronger grape prices”.

The national estimated crush is down 300,000 tonnes (19 per cent) from the 2025 crush and 25 per cent below its 10-year average of 1.69 million tonnes. This equates to around 33 million fewer 9-litre cases of wine.

Peter Bailey, Wine Australia manager for market insights, said that this year’s crush reflected both seasonal pressures and a broader response to changed consumer demand.

“A number of significant seasonal challenges, including flooding in the inland regions, had an impact on production, but the main driver of this lower crush is a deliberate adjustment in response to changing market conditions.

“There have now been four vintages in a row below the long-term average, suggesting an underlying reset in the tonnage of grapes required by winemakers to meet changing global demand,” he said.

Australian winegrape crush 2016-2026.

‘White grapes’ share of crush increases’

Overall, the 2026 crush was 306,334 tonnes smaller than 2025, with red varieties accounting for 80 per cent of the decrease. The crush of red grapes was down by 243,560 tonnes (29 per cent) year-on-year, to be the smallest red crush since 2000.

Conversely, the crush of white grapes declined by a more modest 62,774 tonnes (9 per cent), resulting in a big jump in white’s share of the total crush – up six percentage points to 53 per cent. This is only the second time in the past 12 years that whites have accounted for the majority of the crush.

Bailey said this shift was likely a reflection of changing global consumer preferences toward white wine.

“Globally, red wine has declined at twice the rate of white wine since 2017 and is now nearly 450 million cases lower than it was in that year, according to the IWSR.

“The flow-on effect is that demand for red grapes in Australia has reduced by more than for whites.”

 

‘Reduction in crush is widespread and consistent’

The crush declined in a relatively even manner across all states and major regions.

Bailey noted that over the past five years, the combined crush from the cool/temperate regions has closely matched that of the largest inland region (the South Australian Riverland) – despite the differences in terms of seasonal effects, grape and wine specifications and demand drivers.

“This shows that the global market conditions affect all regions, even if the individual circumstances are different.”

Crush over time from warm inland and cool/temperate regions

‘Grape prices soften further despite small crush’

The average value of purchased grapes in 2026 was $570 per tonne, down 6 per cent compared with 2025. The reduction in overall average purchase value was seen across warm inland and cool / temperate regions, and for both reds and whites. Cool / temperate reds and whites were both down by 3 per cent year-on-year, while across the warm inland regions, reds were down by 1 per cent and whites down by 12 per cent.

Bailey noted that the drop in average values across the board had occurred despite the very low crush size, which is expected to take pressure off wine stocks.

“There has been no improvement in grape prices, which suggests that demand is still very soft.

“It’s concerning that there is no sign of recovery for reds despite such a significant adjustment, and that prices for whites are also now declining.”

Average value for purchases grapes by colour and location over time

Bailey said that the sector will need to consider the size of the supply base that it will require to meet future market demand.

“Current sales of Australian wine on the domestic and export markets equate to around one billion litres, or approximately 1.4 million tonnes,” he explained.

“This could probably be achieved with a total vineyard area of less than 100,000 hectares – about two-thirds of what we estimate our current supply base to be.”

However, he warned that there is also a risk of over-correction because the sector doesn’t yet have reliable data on vineyard plantings and removals.

“When the National Vineyard Register is complete, this will help the sector to make decisions and adjustments that better align supply with demand.”

The National Vintage Report is available on Wine Australia’s website. Vintage survey statistics, including regional winegrape pricing, are available on Wine Australia’s Vintage Survey dashboard. The Grape Price Indicators dashboard for inland regions has also been updated with the latest data. The annual Production, Sales and Inventory Report, which “puts the crush data into the context of wine production and sales and includes a measure of stocks-to-sales ratios”, is published annually in late November.

title: Collaboration the key to unlocking industry recovery, says Will Taylor
summary:
With almost 40 years of wine legal practice, Finlaysons Lawyers chair of partners Will Taylor has witnessed the Australian wine industry through its highs and lows. He says today's conditions are among the toughest he has seen in his career, and that the industry's future may depend on one thing: collaboration. As Taylor puts it, the current state of the industry is “really horrible”. “That doesn’t mean that everybody is doing really badly…but even the people who are doing quite well on the sales front are suffering from much higher input costs than they have in the past, so it’s a real struggle,” he said. While signs of recovery are emerging, Taylor argues that waiting for market conditions to improve won't be enough on its own and that the industry needs a fundamental shift in thinking. “The mindset at the moment in fine wine making land is to control everything, and I think some of those people will have to change mindset…to learn to compromise in order to generate some cash. That's not a criticism, that's a compliment. They need to be perfectionist to make great wine, and that's why we have got such great winemakers in this country…[but] that model is not working anymore, and people are going to be forced to share if they're going to stay in business,” he affirmed.

The post Collaboration the key to unlocking industry recovery, says Will Taylor appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
By Kyra Trussell

With almost 40 years of wine legal practice, Finlaysons Lawyers chair of partners Will Taylor has witnessed the Australian wine industry through its highs and lows. He says today’s conditions are among the toughest he has seen in his career, and that the industry’s future may depend on one thing: collaboration.

Taylor sat down with the Daily Wine News for an exclusive extended interview, where he shared that he views the current state of the industry as “really horrible”.

“That doesn’t mean that everybody is doing really badly…but even the people who are doing quite well on the sales front are suffering from much higher input costs than they have in the past, so it’s a real struggle,” he said.

Although that sounds alarming, Taylor is adamant that there’s been green shoots in the past three months.

He points to the reception of Australian wines at Wine Paris, Treasury Wine Estates’ success in Canada and recent export figures from China as examples of those “little positive signs”.

As for vines being pulled, Taylor deems it a necessary evil for the industry to push forward.

“I hope it’s not going to get any worse, but I think it’s going to be still quite a long, tough haul to get out of this situation. Also, we are finally seeing quite a few vines coming out of the ground. I think that’s tough for the people involved, but I think that’s a good thing for the industry overall – that’s a positive sign.”

While signs of recovery are emerging, Taylor argues that waiting for market conditions to improve won’t be enough on its own and that the industry needs a fundamental shift in thinking.

“The mindset at the moment in fine wine making land is to control everything, and I think some of those people will have to change mindset…to learn to compromise in order to generate some cash.”

“That’s not a criticism, that’s a compliment. They need to be perfectionist to make great wine, and that’s why we have got such great winemakers in this country…[but] that model is not working anymore, and people are going to be forced to share if they’re going to stay in business,” he affirmed.

For Taylor, collaboration within the industry can be as simple as neighbours sharing a tractor or as profound as winemakers sharing entire wineries.

Moreover, he views excess winery infrastructure as one of the industry’s biggest inefficiencies.

“Wineries are very intensive; they’re very capital intensive and very expensive. There’s a lot of very expensive equipment in wineries, and a lot of that equipment is only used for two or three months of the year, and it’s not even fully utilised – it’s not at full capacity.

“That situation is only going to increase as less fruit goes through wineries, as Australia’s vineyard footprint decreases with vines coming out of the ground and quite a lot of fruit not being harvested.

“So, my question is, does a certain wine region need all the wineries in it? The answer is no; it doesn’t. Maybe more people and winemakers work together in less facilities. That’s a big call to make, I know, but if the alternative is going out of business, then that’s the type of thing that needs to happen.”

Another suggestion Taylor has is for wineries to share back-office arrangements including bookkeeping, invoicing, debt collection, export compliance, etc.

“Maybe they could get together and have one back office for a number of wineries that could do all of that for each of the wineries. That involves rationalisation and, unfortunately, involves some people losing their jobs, but the alternative might be that a number of wineries go out of business and a lot more people lose their jobs,” he said.

Moreover, he proposes that joint marketing campaigns could be conducted at a regional level to showcase wineries with different product portfolios at different price points, for example.

The sky is the limit, and Taylor encourages people to “get their thinking caps on and use their imagination”.

“There’s a certain amount of this going on – there just needs to be a lot more of it. For example, there have been some wineries sort of getting together in groups, sort of semi-merging. The Usual Suspects is one collective where a number of wineries joined together, and each has some equity in the overall business.

“There’s jointly owned brands like Mesh, for example, so that’s people collaborating and working together. There are some jointly owned vineyards in Australia, and there’s certainly some wineries that are jointly owned. Barossa Vintage, is a good example of a jointly owned winery.”

For wineries wanting to cut costs without formally merging, Taylor said there are still ways to negotiate and achieve synergies that don’t require official merging, such as the aforementioned resource sharing.

To help wineries navigate these challenges and identify practical opportunities for collaboration, Finlaysons will be travelling across the country for its annual Wine Roadshow.

Finlaysons will be accompanied on the road by Australian Grape & Wine, Wine Australia and wine sector business consultants KPMG.

The program for the 34th annual Finlaysons Wine Roadshow is as follows:

  • Hobart – 3 August 2026
  • Yarra Valley – 4 August 2026
  • Rutherglen – 5 August 2026
  • Riverina – 6 August 2026
  • Hunter Valley – 7 August 2026
  • Barossa – 10 August 2026
  • McLaren Vale – 11 August 2026
  • Riverland – 17 August 2026
  • Limestone Coast – 14 September 2026
  • Margaret River – 30 November 2026

“The stuff we’re going to be talking about is just critical material for wineries wanting to survive and thrive…we’ll be talking about how practically to manage your business to achieve the best financial performance you can in these times, and some of the legal obligations that it’s really important to understand when things are tight,” Taylor shared.

title: Heartland Wines enters administration with $3.6m debt
summary:
According to reporting by The Advertiser, Adelaide-based company Heartland Wines has “collapsed” and owes $3.6 million. The Advertiser claims that a report to creditors revealed that “Westpac provided Heartland with an overdraft and credit card facility that had blown out to $1.2m by mid June”. Heartland reportedly does not produce wine itself and instead outsources production to other companies. Barossa Vintners, a related company storing Heartland’s goods, claims it has a ‘warehouse lien’ – a legal hold giving them the right to the money when the inventory is sold, as detailed by The Advertiser.

The post Heartland Wines enters administration with $3.6m debt appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
According to reporting by The Advertiser, Adelaide-based company Heartland Wines has “collapsed” and owes $3.6 million.

The Advertiser claims that a report to creditors revealed that “Westpac provided Heartland with an overdraft and credit card facility that had blown out to $1.2m by mid June”.

Heartland reportedly does not produce wine itself and instead outsources production to other companies.

Barossa Vintners, a related company storing Heartland’s goods, claims it has a ‘warehouse lien’ – a legal hold giving them the right to the money when the inventory is sold, as detailed by The Advertiser.

The article also says that “Barossa Vintners claims Heartland owes it $1.6m, with roughly $1m of that related to wine production, preservation and storage charges incurred since late 2023”.

Company directors John Collett, Ben Glaetzer and Nick Keukenmeester were contacted by The Advertiser for comment.

Furthermore, The Advertiser notes the report flags a potential insolvent trading claim that would need to be further investigated before pursuit is considered and a potential unfair preference claim against Keukenmeester.

The business continues to trade.

title: Langtons announce Australia’s first-ever Classification of International Wine
summary:
Australia’s fine wine authority Langtons has revealed it will launch its inaugural Classification of International Wine in early August this year. The new classification will be an international version of its Australian Wine classification, which “has been a trusted guide for benchmarking local wines in the fine wine market since 1990”. Langtons said that the purpose of the classification is to provide insights into secondary market trends to assist investors and collectors as well as to act as an authoritative guide for those in search of high-quality international wine. “Australian wine palates are diverse and ever-expanding, with international wines holding an established presence in the collections of the country’s top wine drinkers,” said Tamara Grischy, general manager at Langtons. The classification will feature a collection of 160 wines from across the world’s top wine regions in France, Italy, New Zealand, US, Spain, Portugal and Germany. The organisation said the list has been developed through secondary market auction data collected over the last five years from every international bottle that has been traded through Langtons.

The post Langtons announce Australia’s first-ever Classification of International Wine appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The classification is set to launch in early August this year. Images supplied

Australia’s fine wine authority Langtons has revealed it will launch its inaugural Classification of International Wine in early August this year.

The new classification will be an international version of its Australian Wine classification, which “has been a trusted guide for benchmarking local wines in the fine wine market since 1990”.

Langtons said that the purpose of the classification is to provide insights into secondary market trends to assist investors and collectors as well as to act as an authoritative guide for those in search of high-quality international wine.

“Australian wine palates are diverse and ever-expanding, with international wines holding an established presence in the collections of the country’s top wine drinkers,” said Tamara Grischy, general manager at Langtons.

Tamara Grischy, general manager at Langtons.

“The Langtons Classification of International Wine will be a vital resource for these collectors by highlighting the finest global wines that drive the Australian auction market.

“Our Langtons Classification of Australian Wine has been a trusted source for both producers and drinkers alike for over three decades now, and we’re very excited to impart our expertise to global wines too as the first of its kind in the country.”

The classification will feature a collection of 160 wines from across the world’s top wine regions in France, Italy, New Zealand, US, Spain, Portugal and Germany. The organisation said the list has been developed through secondary market auction data collected over the last five years from every international bottle that has been traded through Langtons.

Just like its Australian counterpart, Langtons said “the Classification of International Wine is not based on opinion, wine show medals or scores, but data reflective of the country’s top fine wine collectors”.

“We look at every bottle of wine internationally that’s sent into Langtons and all its data. This includes aspects such as a wine’s previous bidding history, its clearance rate and changes to its current value versus when the wine was bought,” explained Michael Anderson, head of auctions at Langtons.

Michael Anderson, head of auctions at Langtons

Anderson claimed the classification will feature a mix of hard-to-find cult classics and established titans of the fine wine trade, “indicating new shifts in the fine wine space”.

“Without giving too much away, the wines included in the Classification of International Wine are pointing out some key market trends that will prove valuable to investors and collectors alike.

“Additionally, it’s also showing us what a huge volume of wine is coming through the auction space at the moment, which is a great indicator that the secondary market is strong,” he said.

But Anderson also hinted that some may be surprised with some of the “expected” wines not making an appearance in the inaugural edition – adding that, just because a wine isn’t there initially, doesn’t mean it won’t be featured in the future.

“Scarcity plays a vital role in how the list is made, so if only a barrel is made of a wine, very few people will actually sell it, and if they happen to get their hands on one, they’re more likely to keep it. So that results in less bottles of that particular wine in the auction space and data for us to use.”

While the Classification of International Wine focuses on wines from abroad, Anderson said the initiative also prompts the opportunity for local, Australian wines to shine alongside their global counterparts.

“For example, a wine such as the Bass Phillip Reserve Pinot Noir or Henschke Hill of Grace, both sit easily alongside the greats of Burgundy, Bordeaux and Tuscany. This classification will be a trusted tool to compare wines to see how incredible the quality of our Australian wines are. After all, Langtons was founded on the belief that the best Australian wines have a place at the world’s top tables.”

Langtons said its classifications are inspired by the 1855 Bordeaux Classification, which saw wines from the region ranked in importance from first to fifth growth crus mainly based on selling history.

The Langtons Classification of International Wine will see all wines categorised as ‘Classified’. This differs from the Classification of Australian Wine, which sees wines categorised as ‘Classified’, along with a small number deemed a higher ‘First Classified’ status.

title: 2026 Vaughn Dell Scholarship recipient announced
summary:
Georgie Jacobs, vineyard manager at Tolpuddle Vineyard, has been revealed as the inaugural recipient of the $10,000 Vaughn Dell Scholarship. Jacobs will travel to the USA, where she will investigate practical adoption of regenerative practices in vineyards. She will focus on under vine management, cool climate biological farming, increasing soil biology and fungal populations while addressing soil compaction and reducing reliance on herbicides. Supported by Linda Morice and the Dell family, the new scholarship was launched to build on Vaughn’s legacy and “continue elevating the Tasmanian wine offering”. Jacobs said that she feels very humbled to receive the scholarship.

The post 2026 Vaughn Dell Scholarship recipient announced appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Georgie Jacobs, the inaugural recipient of the scholarship. Images supplied

Georgie Jacobs, vineyard manager at Tolpuddle Vineyard, has been revealed as the inaugural recipient of the $10,000 Vaughn Dell Scholarship.

Jacobs will travel to the USA, where she will investigate practical adoption of regenerative practices in vineyards. She will focus on under vine management, cool climate biological farming, increasing soil biology and fungal populations while addressing soil compaction and reducing reliance on herbicides.

Supported by Linda Morice and the Dell family, the new scholarship was launched to build on Vaughn’s legacy and “continue elevating the Tasmanian wine offering”.

Jacobs said that she feels very humbled to receive the scholarship.

“It’s an honour to be selected to represent our wine community, and I’m grateful for the opportunity to travel and dive into a research topic I’m passionate about. I will travel to the USA and road trip along the West Coast to visit and meet producers who are innovating in regenerative viticulture. With cool-climate and low rainfall wine regions there are many parallels to our Tasmanian conditions, and I’m interested in how growers are adopting biological strategies.

“Growing wine in places of natural significance is a privilege, and I believe it comes with a responsibility to be proactive in our farming decisions. I hope to discover new ways to practically and realistically make real change in our vineyards. I’m very grateful to Linda Morice and the Dell family for this opportunity, and hope that I can do justice to Vaughn’s curiosity and love of travel and learning. Many thanks also to Wine Tasmania, the Tasmanian Government and the team at Tolpuddle Vineyard for their support.”

Linda Morice expressed her support for Jacobs’ selection.

“Georgie’s project captures so much of what Vaughn believed in. He was endlessly curious and never content to accept conventional thinking if there was a better way. He believed the best ideas came from asking questions, learning from others, and then generously sharing that knowledge with the wider wine community.

“Georgie’s focus on exploring practical approaches to regenerative viticulture reflects that same spirit of innovation and continual learning. Just as importantly, Georgie has the ability to bring those learnings home and share them in a way that will inspire and benefit others across Tasmania’s wine community. That commitment to learning, collaboration and knowledge-sharing is exactly the kind of legacy we hoped the Vaughn Dell Scholarship would carry forward,” Morice said.

Wine Tasmania CEO Sheralee Davies also welcomed the announcement.

“We were delighted to see the quality and number of applications for this new scholarship program, reflecting the enthusiasm of wine producers to keep learning, continuously improving, collaborating and sharing their knowledge for the benefit of all.

“There were many worthwhile applications, and it wasn’t an easy process for the selection panel. Georgie’s proposal is very practical and relevant, focused on achievable outcomes, and clearly outlines how these outcomes will be shared for the benefit of the Tassie wine sector more broadly. It also aligns with Vaughn’s focus on sustainability in viticulture,” Davies said.

The Tasmanian wine community lost a much loved and respected friend when Vaughn suddenly passed away on 19 May 2020 at just 39 years of age.

The late Vaughn Dell, whom the scholarship honours. 

Wine Tasmania added that, together with Linda Morice, Vaughn’s relentless desire to learn and excel is reflected in the meticulous Sinapius vineyard and wines.

“Never one to do things the easy way or follow others, Vaughn’s curiosity, positive attitude, determination and generosity were an inspiration for many,” the organisation said.

Applications were invited from Tasmanian wine professionals to undertake study-based travel in 2026/27 on topics of relevance and benefit for both the individual and the broader Tasmanian wine sector.

The scholarship is supported by the state government.

title: ASVO scholarship recipients to bring insights home for wine sector
summary:
This August, Tasmania’s wine producers are heading interstate for two exclusive trade tastings in Sydney and Melbourne. Hosted by Wine Tasmania at Chin Chin Sydney on Monday 24 August and Chin Chin Melbourne on Monday 31 August, the afternoon tastings will showcase “some of Tasmania's most exciting producers to the country’s key wine trade buyers”. “From world-renowned, razor-sharp sparkling wines to elegant Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, Riesling and emerging cool climate varieties, attendees will have the opportunity to taste their way across Tasmania's regional wine areas – all in one room,” said Wine Tasmania. CEO of Wine Tasmania Sheralee Davies said the events are a key opportunity to connect Tasmanian producers with some of the country's most influential wine professionals.

The post ASVO scholarship recipients to bring insights home for wine sector appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Syuzanna Mosikyan, Song (Sai) Yan and Liz Riley with Jerome Scarborough. Images supplied

The Australian Society of Viticulture and Oenology (ASVO) has announced the recipients of its 2026 ASVO Scholarships to be Song Yan (Next Gen Scholarship), Syuzanna Mosikyan (Growth Scholarship) and Liz Riley as well as Jerome Scarborough (Dr Patrick Iland Scholarship).

“We are proud to support these individuals with their research which advances excellence across our sector, builds capability for future innovation, and creates useful resources to share with our ASVO community and beyond,” said Emily White, ASVO executive director.

The ASVO Next Gen Scholarship, which is supported by Wine Australia with levies from Australia’s grapegrowers and winemakers plus matching funds from the federal government, was awarded to Song (Sai) Yan who is a PhD candidate at Adelaide University “evaluating the potential for smoke from prescribed burning to taint grapes and wine”.

Yan will use the $1,000 scholarship to support his participation as a speaker at the 2026 Wine Active Compounds – In Vino Analytica Scientia (WAC–IVAS) conference in Dijon. He will also visit the Institut des Sciences de la Vigne et du Vin (ISVV) at the University of Bordeaux in France for international knowledge exchange.

“I’m honoured to receive the ASVO NextGen Scholarship. This support will enable me to present my research at the WAC–IVAS 2026 conference in France. I look forward to strengthening international collaborations, exchanging ideas with researchers and industry professionals, and bringing new knowledge back to benefit the Australian wine sector,” Yan said.

Jo Hargeaves, senior program manager at Wine Australia, said the organisations support of the scholarship helps talented emerging researchers like Yan access world-class expertise.

“By investing in their development, we’re strengthening the future capability of researchers working on challenges facing the sector and ensuring valuable insights are shared back with industry,” said Hargeaves.

Syuzanna Mosikyan, who is in the final stages of her PhD, was awarded the Growth Scholarship. She will use the $2,500 toward the next part of her ongoing research project on regulations and industry readiness for gene-edited grape varieties in Australia and Europe.

“I am delighted to receive the ASVO Growth Scholarship and grateful for ASVO’s continued support of my professional development. This opportunity builds on my previous ASVO-supported research trip to Canberra and my current OIV-funded Australia-France research.

“It will help me continue examining how new breeding techniques can contribute to broader conversations around sustainability, regulation, biodiversity, and climate adaptation in wine, with the aim of translating these learnings into practical insights for the Australian wine industry,” said Mosikyan.

The Dr Patrick Iland Scholarship, which is supported by Dr Iland and co-authors of his renowned technical books, will see Liz Riley and Jerome Scarborough jointly receive $5,000 toward their trip to France and Italy to look at the adoption of disease resistant grape varieties.

The ASVO said the pair will visit growers who adopted commercially bred Chardonnay and Pinot disease-resistant crosses at harvest time to assess their growth habits, bunch architecture, flavour and pre-harvest attributes, with a view to assessing their suitability for commercial adoption in Australia.

“We would like to thank both the ASVO and Patrick Iland and his co-authors who support this opportunity for ASVO members to participate in a non-traditional professional development opportunity,” Riley said.

The ASVO added that recipients will report on their experiences and outcomes through ASVO communications channels, ensuring the broader community benefits from their insights.

title: Registrations open for the French-Victorian wine show
summary:
Registrations are now open for the 2026 Air Liquide Concours des Vins – The French-Victorian Wine Show, inviting Victorian wineries to submit their wines “for assessment by an esteemed panel of French, Australian and international wine professionals”. Hosted by FACCI with support of naming rights sponsor Air Liquide, the Concours des Vins “celebrates the quality, diversity and craftsmanship of Victorian winemaking through a distinctive French-Australian lens”. The event has partnered with Sommeliers Australia this year, which organisers said further strengthens the competition’s “connection with Australia’s wine service and sommelier community”.The two judging panels will be chaired by Sarah Andrew DipWSET, founder and director of Selador Wines as well as consultant, sommelier and educator. To enter, wines must be produced from Victorian vineyards. Eligible categories include Aromatic Whites (2025 vintage), Chardonnay, Pinot Noir and Shiraz (2024 or 2025 vintages).

The post Registrations open for the French-Victorian wine show appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The judging panels will be chaired by Sarah Andrew, pictured. Image supplied

Registrations are now open for the 2026 Air Liquide Concours des Vins – The French-Victorian Wine Show, inviting Victorian wineries to submit their wines “for assessment by an esteemed panel of French, Australian and international wine professionals”.

Hosted by FACCI with support of naming rights sponsor Air Liquide, the Concours des Vins “celebrates the quality, diversity and craftsmanship of Victorian winemaking through a distinctive French-Australian lens”.

The event has partnered with Sommeliers Australia this year, which organisers said further strengthens the competition’s “connection with Australia’s wine service and sommelier community”.

The two judging panels will be chaired by Sarah Andrew DipWSET, founder and director of Selador Wines as well as consultant, sommelier and educator. She will be joined by seven wine professionals spanning winemaking, sommelier service, education, consulting, import and distribution: Sophie Carbonneau, Thomas Gisbert, Gilles Lapalus, Loic Le Calvez, Christian Maier, Alexandre Rougeot and Camm Whiteoak. Sommeliers Australia will also appoint an associate judge.

“We set up the two panels with diverse skill sets to ensure each includes a winemaker for technical expertise alongside the stylistic perspectives of sommeliers, educators, consultants, importers and distributors.

“Five of the judges are French nationals, which is a point of difference for the show. They all come from the top of their industries,” said Sarah Andrew.

To enter, wines must be produced from Victorian vineyards. Eligible categories include Aromatic Whites (2025 vintage), Chardonnay, Pinot Noir and Shiraz (2024 or 2025 vintages).

All wines will be judged blind within their category using the internationally recognised 100-point scoring system, with medal results announced in mid-October 2026.

“In the glass, we are looking for varietal definition, balance above all, as well as quality and depth. Each wine is judged on its own merit using the 100-point international system,” said Andrew.

The two highest-scoring medal-winning wines from each category will be showcased at the Gala Dinner and Special Awards Ceremony on Friday 13 November 2026 at Sofitel Melbourne on Collins. The evening will also feature the announcement of the ‘Wine of the Year’, selected by the jury, and the ‘Public Choice Trophy’, voted by Gala Dinner guests. Both winners will receive “in-depth French Wine education courses” from Cardwell Cellars.

“Winning Wine of the Year at the Concours des Vins was an incredible honour for our business. Not only did it provide valuable exposure for our wines, but it also gave us the opportunity to connect with other passionate winemakers and industry professionals,” said Quinn Livingstone, owner of Harcourt Valley Vineyards and 2025 Wine of the Year winner.

Entries close Monday 31 August 2026 at 5pm.

Full entry criteria, eligible vintages, registration fees and delivery instructions are available here.

title: Wine Tasmania to host exclusive trade tastings in Sydney and Melbourne
summary:
This August, Tasmania’s wine producers are heading interstate for two exclusive trade tastings in Sydney and Melbourne. Hosted by Wine Tasmania at Chin Chin Sydney on Monday 24 August and Chin Chin Melbourne on Monday 31 August, the afternoon tastings will showcase “some of Tasmania's most exciting producers to the country’s key wine trade buyers”. “From world-renowned, razor-sharp sparkling wines to elegant Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, Riesling and emerging cool climate varieties, attendees will have the opportunity to taste their way across Tasmania's regional wine areas – all in one room,” said Wine Tasmania. CEO of Wine Tasmania Sheralee Davies said the events are a key opportunity to connect Tasmanian producers with some of the country's most influential wine professionals.

The post Wine Tasmania to host exclusive trade tastings in Sydney and Melbourne appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The tastings are strictly limited to professional wine trade. Image supplied.

This August, Tasmania’s wine producers are heading interstate for two exclusive trade tastings in Sydney and Melbourne.

Hosted by Wine Tasmania at Chin Chin Sydney on Monday 24 August and Chin Chin Melbourne on Monday 31 August, the afternoon tastings will showcase “some of Tasmania’s most exciting producers to the country’s key wine trade buyers”.

“From world-renowned, razor-sharp sparkling wines to elegant Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, Riesling and emerging cool climate varieties, attendees will have the opportunity to taste their way across Tasmania’s regional wine areas – all in one room,” said Wine Tasmania.

CEO of Wine Tasmania Sheralee Davies said the events are a key opportunity to connect Tasmanian producers with some of the country’s most influential wine professionals.

“Tasmanian wine is all about quality over quantity – which the low-yielding 2026 vintage has certainly demonstrated. Global demand for the island’s wines continues to grow, and this is a rare chance for wine trade representatives to try an array of limited-availability wines in one place.

“Beyond just an (excellent) tasting, these events are an opportunity for wine trade to meet the growers and makers, hear the stories behind the wines and each vintage, and discover what makes Tasmania one of the world’s most exciting cool climate wine regions,” she said.

Wine Tasmania said the interstate tasting events build on its “hugely popular national trade visitation program”, which brings “influential buyers and decision-makers to the island each year to experience first-hand the depth and diversity of the Tasmanian wine landscape”.

The tastings are strictly limited to professional wine trade.

Details for Sydney are available here, whilst information on the Melbourne event can be found here.

title: Coonawarra’s Bowen Estate celebrates 50th vintage milestone
summary:
Bowen Estate in Coonawarra is celebrating its 50th vintage this month, an achievement that Coonawarra Vignerons says shows the enduring commitment of a family whose name has become synonymous with classic Coonawarra Cabernet Sauvignon. Family owned and operated since its foundation in 1975 by Doug and Joy Bowen, the winery “has remained true to its philosophy of crafting wines that faithfully express the unique character of Coonawarra's celebrated terra rossa soils”. Reflecting on his journey, Doug Bowen said that he never wanted to work for anyone else. “I had lots of energy and passion to start my own winery experience. I never wanted to work for anyone else, simply wanted to put all my efforts into my own project.” Doug had studied agriculture before completing Oenology at Roseworthy Agricultural College, but it was one unforgettable bottle of wine that cemented his future. “I was fortunate in the late ’60s to share a bottle of Mildara Coonawarra Cabernet 1962. It was a moment in my life I will always remember. My passion and belief in Coonawarra was born there and then.” That conviction led Doug and Joy to establish Bowen Estate, believing the region’s famed terra rossa soils could produce wines of exceptional character and longevity.

The post Coonawarra’s Bowen Estate celebrates 50th vintage milestone appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Doug Bowen and his daughter Emma Bowen. Images supplied.

Bowen Estate in Coonawarra is celebrating its 50th vintage this month, an achievement that Coonawarra Vignerons says shows the enduring commitment of a family whose name has become synonymous with classic Coonawarra Cabernet Sauvignon.

Family owned and operated since its foundation in 1975 by Doug and Joy Bowen, the winery “has remained true to its philosophy of crafting wines that faithfully express the unique character of Coonawarra’s celebrated terra rossa soils”.

Reflecting on his journey, Doug Bowen said that he never wanted to work for anyone else.

“I had lots of energy and passion to start my own winery experience. I never wanted to work for anyone else, simply wanted to put all my efforts into my own project.”

Bowen Estate in the 1970’s.

Doug had studied agriculture before completing Oenology at Roseworthy Agricultural College, but it was one unforgettable bottle of wine that cemented his future.

“I was fortunate in the late ’60s to share a bottle of Mildara Coonawarra Cabernet 1962. It was a moment in my life I will always remember. My passion and belief in Coonawarra was born there and then.”

That conviction led Doug and Joy to establish Bowen Estate, believing the region’s famed terra rossa soils could produce wines of exceptional character and longevity.

Doug and Joy believed from the start that great wine begins in the vineyard, with Coonawarra Vignerons highlighting their “hands-on approach, attention to detail and unwavering belief in the quality of their fruit” as defining characteristics of the winery.

“To make the best wine you have to have the best grapes. End of story,” said Doug.

That belief continues to hold up today, where the couple’s daughter Emma Bowen leads the winemaking with support from Doug.

“With Emma in the winery I am immensely proud of what she is doing and has achieved.

“I have stepped back from the winery largely over the past few vintages and she has accepted the new responsibility well. Fortunately, we both share the same philosophy and commitment to our style,” he said.

For Emma, the 50th milestone is as much about honouring the generations before her as it is about looking towards the future.

“Bowen Estate has never just been where I work—it’s where I grew up. Vintage was part of our family life, and some of my fondest memories are of watching my parents in the winery and vineyard.

“To now stand beside Dad as we celebrate and release our 50th vintage is incredibly humbling. It represents everything my parents have built over the past five decades, and I’m honoured to help carry that legacy forward,” she said.

To celebrate the occasion, exclusive trade tastings have been hosted by Emma Bowen in Sydney and Melbourne, introducing the milestone release while sharing the family’s story with leading members of the Australian wine trade.

Moreover, visitors to the Bowen Estate cellar door this month will be among the first to experience the landmark 50th vintage before its broader release.

Coonawarra Vignerons extended congratulations to the Bowen family on the milestone, calling it a “remarkable achievement and a fitting celebration of one of Coonawarra’s great family wine stories”.
